_meta.note: Quiz-genre tone · curly apostrophes only · glossary locked

title_001: The Analects — Dopamine Master Quiz · Confucius
html_001: Confucius · The first Confucian classic · 20 books
html_002: The oldest conversation about character and learning
html_004: “Is it not a joy to learn, and to practice what you learn in season?”
html_005: — Confucius · The Analects · Book 1 (Xue Er, 學而)
html_006: The core ideas, concepts, and themes Confucius and his students traded in this book —
html_008:
. You start easy and it gets harder step by step,
        and
html_009: wrong questions come back until you get them right.
html_026: That’s the end of the free preview
html_029: A junzi who sees the Way
html_042: Thread it all onto and you’ll never forget it. Every starting point is
html_047: The joy of learning and practicing in season
html_049: ❤️ Benevolence (仁) — a heart that loves people
html_050: Start with filial love close by, then widen it
html_052: 🎎 Practice it in relationships through ritual (禮)
html_053: The form in which your heart shows outward
html_055: 🧑‍🎓 The junzi (君子) — you become a person of virtue
html_056: A character that chases rightness (義) over profit
html_058: ⭐ Rule by virtue (德治) — the world grows benevolent
html_059: “Virtue gathers people like the North Star”
html_061: Table of contents at a glance
html_063: 3 cases from the book worth pondering
html_065: Focused review of what you missed
html_067: you can explore Confucius, Confucianism, and the core ideas of The Analects in more depth.
html_073: A repeat-master quiz that burns a book’s core into you with dopamine
html_074: This app is secondary study content that reworks the book’s core ideas for learning. For the full reading experience, please meet the original book itself.

js_004: Who is the central figure behind the words and deeds in The Analects?
js_007: Sun Wu, author of The Art of War
js_009: The Analects gathers the words and deeds of Confucius (551–479 BCE), a thinker of the Spring and Autumn period, and his students. It’s the first Confucian classic and a root of the East Asian mind.
js_011: What kind of book is The Analects?
js_012: A record of the words and deeds of Confucius and his students
js_013: A history that Confucius wrote and published himself
js_014: A law code laying out the state’s laws and systems
js_015: A liturgy setting out rites of sacrifice to Heaven
js_016: The Analects isn’t something Confucius wrote himself — it’s a record his students compiled from their teacher’s words and deeds. That’s why it runs as short exchanges and dialogues, one fragment after another.
js_018: How did The Analects come to take its present form?
js_019: After Confucius died, his students and later scholars gathered his words and compiled them
js_020: Confucius took up the brush while alive and edited and published it himself
js_021: A later emperor ordered his officials to compose it anew
js_022: A single student wrote it all down alone
js_023: After Confucius died, The Analects was edited over a long time, passing through the hands of many students and later scholars. So each book has a slightly different texture, and the same themes recur.
js_025: How many books make up the Analects we have today?
js_030: The Analects is divided into 20 books. Each title is just the first couple of characters of its opening line, so the titles don’t carry deep meaning on their own (e.g., Book 1, “Xue Er”).
js_032: In what era and state did Confucius live?
js_033: The state of Lu, Spring and Autumn period
js_034: The state of Qin, Warring States period
js_037: Confucius lived in the state of Lu in the Spring and Autumn period, a chaotic age as the Zhou order collapsed. Setting a disordered world right through ritual (禮) and virtue (德) was his lifelong aim.
js_039: Which virtue did Confucius prize most in life?
js_041: Hegemony that crushes others by force
js_043: A drive to win out over others
js_044: At the heart of Confucius’ thought sits ren (仁). In a word, ren is “a heart that loves people” — the highest virtue running through the whole of The Analects.
js_046: Where does the joy in the opening line of The Analects come from?
js_047: From learning and practicing it in season
js_048: From endlessly fighting others and finally winning
js_049: From piling up wealth in your storehouse
js_051: “Is it not a joy to learn and practice in season? (學而時習之不亦說乎)” — this opening line, beginning with the joy of learning, opens the door to the whole book.
js_053: “Do not do to others what you would not want done to you” — in one character, this teaching is?
js_057: “Do not impose on others what you yourself do not want (己所不欲勿施於人)” — this heart, refusing to do to others what you’d hate, is called shu (恕). Confucius named this single character a word to live by for life.
js_059: What name did Confucius give to his ideal human being?
js_064: The ideal human Confucius dreamed of is the junzi (君子). Once the word meant “a ruler’s son (a noble),” but Confucius remade it into a matter of character — “a person of virtue.”
js_066: What heart, turned toward one’s parents, did Confucius see as the root of ren (仁)?
js_068: Loyalty to the ruler and state (忠)
js_071: Confucius saw ren (仁) as learning love from filial piety (孝) close at hand and steadily widening it. The heart turned toward your parents is the starting point of all benevolence.
js_080: Confucius (551–479 BCE) was a man of Lu in the Spring and Autumn period, a thinker who tried to set a collapsing world right through ritual and virtue. His words and life survive as The Analects, through his students’ records.
js_081: The Analects isn’t a systematic treatise but a collection of short exchanges and anecdotes. Fragments beginning “The Master said (子曰)” follow one another — a book you read by chewing over one passage at a time.
js_082: The Analects was compiled by students and later scholars over many generations after Confucius died. So the style and concerns shift from one book to another, and some parts are thought to be later additions.
js_083: The Analects splits into 20 books, each named after the first few characters of its opening line (Xue Er, Wei Zheng, Ba Yi…). So you can’t tell a book’s theme from its title alone.
js_084: The Spring and Autumn period Confucius lived in was a chaotic age when the Zhou royal house weakened and the lords fought among themselves. He sought to revive the old Zhou order of ritual and music and steady the world.
js_085: Ren (仁) is the highest virtue in The Analects. It’s explained in many ways depending on the situation, but the root always gathers into “a heart that cherishes and loves people.”
js_086: The first book, “Xue Er” (學而), opens with “Learn and practice it in season” and sings of the joy of learning, friendship, and self-cultivation. It’s like an overture that distills what The Analects means to say.
js_087: “Do not do to others what you would not want done to you” is the Confucian golden rule. The consideration (恕) of not doing to others what you’d hate is the most concrete way to practice ren (仁) day to day.
js_088: Junzi (君子) once meant the ruling class, but Confucius recast it as “a character of virtue.” The point is that anyone can become a junzi — not by birth, but through learning and self-cultivation.
js_089: Confucius held that you learn love from filial piety (孝) toward your closest kin, your parents, and widen it out to siblings, neighbors, and the world. So filial piety is both the first step toward ren and its root.
js_103: The highest virtue in Confucius’ thought. In a word, ai ren (愛人) — a heart that cherishes and loves people — and it runs through the whole of The Analects.
js_105: The forms and bearing that carry the way between people. It’s the path by which the ren within shows outward, so ren and ritual pair up as inside and out.
js_106: Reciprocity (恕) — what you hate, spare others too
js_107: Do not do to others what you would not want. This consideration — never doing to others what you’d hate — is the most concrete way to practice ren in daily life.
js_108: Junzi (君子) — a person of virtue
js_109: An ideal character reached not by birth but through learning and self-cultivation. The one who chases rightness (義) before profit is a junzi.
js_110: Rectifying names (正名) — live up to your name
js_111: Let the ruler be a ruler, the minister a minister. A political philosophy: the world stands right when each person acts fitting their place.
js_112: Filial piety (孝) and brotherliness — the root of ren
js_113: It starts with love for your closest parents and widens to siblings, neighbors, and the world. Filial and brotherly love is the very root of practicing ren.
js_114: Learning (學) — the joy of studying and practicing
js_115: Learn and practice in season, and it’s a joy. Learning and thinking must go together, and the attitude is wen gu zhi xin — reviewing the old to know the new.
js_116: Rule by virtue (德治) — the politics of the North Star
js_117: Govern by virtue. Rule with virtue and people gather as all the stars turn to the North Star. It’s virtue, not force or punishment, that moves the world.
js_119: Book “Xue Er” (學而) — the gate of learning and character
js_120: It opens the book with “Is it not a joy to learn and practice in season?”
js_121: It sings of the delight of friends coming from afar.
js_122: It says filial piety and respect (孝悌) are the root of practicing ren.
js_123: It sets the habit of daily self-reflection (examining yourself three times a day).
js_125: Ren (仁) — a heart that loves people
js_126: What is ren? The answer is ai ren (愛人): to love people.
js_127: The keji fuli taught to Yan Yuan: mastering yourself and returning to ritual is ren.
js_128: Do not do to others what you would not want — you don’t inflict on others what you’d hate.
js_129: Ren isn’t far off — it’s right beside you the moment you decide to do it.
js_131: Ritual (禮) and rectifying names — the order of relationships
js_132: Ritual is the form and bearing in which the ren within shows outward.
js_133: Ruler as ruler, minister as minister — this is rectifying names, living up to them.
js_134: Only when name and reality match do words hold and things get done.
js_135: Ritual is the path that reduces strife between people and builds harmony.
js_137: Learning (學) — the path of study and knowing
js_138: Learn without thinking and you’re in the dark; think without learning and you’re in peril — so the two go together.
js_139: Where three walk together, one is surely my teacher.
js_140: It takes the attitude of wen gu zhi xin — reviewing the old to know the new.
js_141: To call what you know known, and what you don’t unknown — that is true knowing.
js_143: The junzi — one who chases rightness
js_144: An ideal character reached not by birth but through learning and self-cultivation.
js_145: The junzi is clear on rightness; the petty person is clear on profit.
js_146: The junzi harmonizes without conforming — in tune, yet never losing their center.
js_147: They look for the fault in themselves, not in others.
js_150: Govern by virtue — it likens virtuous rule to the North Star.
js_151: Rather than frightening people with punishment, it makes them know shame through virtue and ritual.
js_152: Governance that puts those near at ease and draws those far away to come.
js_153: Cultivating yourself (self-discipline) and putting the people at peace — that’s the end of governance.
js_154: The keji fuli taught to Yan Yuan
js_155: When his foremost student Yan Yuan asked about ren, Confucius answered: mastering yourself and returning to ritual is ren (克己復禮爲仁). Do so even for a day and the world turns to ren — for ren comes from you, not from others.
js_156: It brings benevolence down from a grand ideal to a matter of your own restraint and choice. The insight: the first step to changing the world lies in mastering your own desires.
js_157: The one character given to Zigong: shu (恕)
js_158: When Zigong asked for one word to live by all his life, Confucius answered: it is shu (恕) — do not do to others what you do not want (己所不欲勿施於人). A vast teaching gathers into this single sentence.
js_159: It’s the Confucian version of the golden rule that East and West both discovered. It compresses a tangled morality into one sentence anyone can put into practice today.
js_160: A trail of growth, marked by age
js_161: Confucius summed up his own life like this: at fifteen I set my heart on learning; at thirty I stood firm; at forty I was free of doubts; at fifty I knew the will of Heaven; at sixty my ear was attuned; at seventy I could follow my heart’s desire without overstepping the line.
js_162: It shows that arrival never comes all at once — it’s the fruit of a lifetime of learning and cultivation. Even a sage grew step by step: a comfort and a signpost at once.
js_163: Am I doing to others, without realizing it, what I’d hate to have done to me?
js_164: Bring to mind one recent conflict, and think about what would change if you applied shu (恕) — weighing things from the other person’s side — in that moment.
js_165: Between profit (利) and rightness (義), which do I chase first?
js_166: Set side by side a moment you chose rightness even at a loss, and one where you did the opposite. By that choice Confucius divided the junzi from the petty person.
js_167: Am I only learning and skipping thought — or only thinking and skipping learning?
js_168: Reflect on whether you’ve just been piling up information lately, or judging without digesting — then find a way to fill in whichever side is missing.
js_169: 🧑‍🎓 A junzi who sees the Way
js_170: You read all the way to the deep meaning of The Analects
js_172: You’ve caught almost all the core
js_173: 🌱 Someone who has set their heart on learning
js_174: You’ve got the framework — one more round!
js_175: 🚪 Someone who just opened the first gate of learning
js_176: The more you repeat, the higher your score climbs

_app.title: The Analects Master Quiz
_app.desc: Burn the core of Confucius’ Analects into you with a dopamine repeat quiz
